- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- gsd-advisor-researcher: Researches a single gray area decision and returns a structured comparison table with rationale. Spawned by discuss-phase advisor mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-ai-researcher: Researches a chosen AI framework's official docs to produce implementation-ready guidance — best practices, syntax, core patterns, and pitfalls distilled for the specific use case. Writes the Framework Quick Reference and Implementation Guidance sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)
- gsd-assumptions-analyzer: Deeply analyzes codebase for a phase and returns structured assumptions with evidence. Spawned by discuss-phase assumptions mode. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-fixer: Applies fixes to code review findings from REVIEW.md. Reads source files, applies intelligent fixes, and commits each fix atomically. Spawned by /gsd-code-review --fix. (Tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-code-reviewer: Reviews source files for bugs, security issues, and code quality problems. Produces structured REVIEW.md with severity-classified findings. Spawned by /gsd-code-review. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-codebase-mapper: Explores codebase and writes structured analysis documents. Spawned by map-codebase with a focus area (tech, arch, quality, concerns). Writes documents directly to reduce orchestrator context load. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Skill)

- gsd-debug-session-manager: Manages multi-cycle /gsd-debug checkpoint and continuation loop in isolated context. Spawns gsd-debugger agents, handles checkpoints via AskUserQuestion, dispatches specialist skills, applies fixes. Returns compact summary to main context. Spawned by /gsd-debug command. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Agent, AskUserQuestion)

- gsd-debugger: Investigates bugs using scientific method, manages debug sessions, handles checkpoints. Spawned by /gsd-debug orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch)

- gsd-doc-classifier: Classifies a single planning document as ADR, PRD, SPEC, DOC, or UNKNOWN. Extracts title, scope summary, and cross-references. Spawned in parallel by /gsd-ingest-docs. Writes a JSON classification file and returns a one-line confirmation. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-synthesizer: Synthesizes classified planning docs into a single consolidated context. Applies precedence rules, detects cross-ref cycles, enforces LOCKED-vs-LOCKED hard-blocks, and writes INGEST-CONFLICTS.md with three buckets (auto-resolved, competing-variants, unresolved-blockers). Spawned by /gsd-ingest-docs. (Tools: Read, Write, Grep, Glob, Bash)
- gsd-doc-verifier: Verifies factual claims in generated docs against the live codebase. Returns structured JSON per doc. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob)

- gsd-doc-writer: Writes and updates project documentation. Spawned with a doc_assignment block specifying doc type, mode (create/update/supplement), and project context. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Skill)

- gsd-domain-researcher: Researches the business domain and real-world application context of the AI system being built. Surfaces domain expert evaluation criteria, industry-specific failure modes, regulatory context, and what "good" looks like for practitioners in this field — before the eval-planner turns it into measurable rubrics. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-eval-auditor: Retroactive audit of an implemented AI phase's evaluation coverage. Checks implementation against the AI-SPEC.md evaluation plan. Scores each eval dimension as COVERED/PARTIAL/MISSING. Produces a scored EVAL-REVIEW.md with findings, gaps, and remediation guidance. Spawned by /gsd-eval-review orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- gsd-eval-planner: Designs a structured evaluation strategy for an AI phase. Identifies critical failure modes, selects eval dimensions with rubrics, recommends tooling, and specifies the reference dataset. Writes the Evaluation Strategy, Guardrails, and Production Monitoring sections of AI-SPEC.md. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, AskUserQuestion)

- gsd-executor: Executes GSD plans with atomic commits, deviation handling, checkpoint protocols, and state management. Spawned by execute-phase orchestrator or execute-plan command. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-framework-selector: Presents an interactive decision matrix to surface the right AI/LLM framework for the user's specific use case. Produces a scored recommendation with rationale. Spawned by /gsd-ai-integration-phase and /gsd-select-framework orchestrators. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebSearch, AskUserQuestion)
- gsd-integration-checker: Verifies cross-phase integration and E2E flows. Checks that phases connect properly and user workflows complete end-to-end. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)
- gsd-intel-updater: Analyzes codebase and writes structured intel files to .planning/intel/. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep)

- gsd-mempalace-curator: Ship-time MemPalace curation — writes the session diary, proposes/creates cross-project tunnels, mirrors extract-learnings into the temporal KG, and runs wing-scoped drawer pruning. Spawned at ship:post by the mempalace capability. (Tools: Read, Bash, Grep, Glob)
- gsd-nyquist-auditor: Fills Nyquist validation gaps by generating tests and verifying coverage for phase requirements (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-pattern-mapper: Analyzes codebase for existing patterns and produces PATTERNS.md mapping new files to closest analogs. Read-only codebase analysis spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator before planning. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Write)

- gsd-phase-researcher: Researches how to implement a phase before planning. Produces RESEARCH.md consumed by gsd-planner. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*, mcp__perplexity__*)
- gsd-plan-checker: Verifies plans will achieve phase goal before execution. Goal-backward analysis of plan quality. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-planner: Creates executable phase plans with task breakdown, dependency analysis, and goal-backward verification. Spawned by /gsd-plan-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*)

- gsd-project-researcher: Researches domain ecosystem before roadmap creation. Produces files in .planning/research/ consumed during roadmap creation. Spawned by /gsd-new-project or /gsd-new-milestone orchestrators. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*, mcp__perplexity__*)

- gsd-research-synthesizer: Synthesizes research outputs from parallel researcher agents into SUMMARY.md. Spawned by /gsd-new-project after 4 researcher agents complete. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Skill)

- gsd-roadmapper: Creates project roadmaps with phase breakdown, requirement mapping, success criteria derivation, and coverage validation. Spawned by /gsd-new-project orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-security-auditor: Verifies threat mitigations from PLAN.md threat model exist in implemented code. Returns structured security verdict (SECURED / OPEN_THREATS / ESCALATE). Spawned by /gsd-secure-phase. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)
- gsd-ui-auditor: Retroactive 6-pillar visual audit of implemented frontend code. Produces scored UI-REVIEW.md. Spawned by /gsd-ui-review orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)
- gsd-ui-checker: Validates UI-SPEC.md design contracts against 6 quality dimensions. Produces BLOCK/FLAG/PASS verdicts. Spawned by /gsd-ui-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Bash, Glob, Grep, Skill)

- gsd-ui-researcher: Produces UI-SPEC.md design contract for frontend phases. Reads upstream artifacts, detects design system state, asks only unanswered questions. Spawned by /gsd-ui-phase orchestrator. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill, WebSearch, WebFetch, mcp__context7__*, mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*, mcp__firecrawl__*, mcp__exa__*, mcp__tavily__*, mcp__ref__*, mcp__jina__*)

- gsd-user-profiler: Analyzes extracted session messages across 8 behavioral dimensions to produce a scored developer profile with confidence levels and evidence. Spawned by profile orchestration workflows. (Tools: Read)

- gsd-verifier: Verifies phase goal achievement through goal-backward analysis. Checks codebase delivers what phase promised, not just that tasks completed. Creates VERIFICATION.md report. (Tools: Read, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob, Skill)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

Use whenever tagging a version, running `gh release create`, or shipping a release on such a repo — the default paths create lightweight tags that these backends resolve to a wrong, dev-suffixed version. Creates an annotated tag via the git data API and verifies the result.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

lastPrompt: I ran "pc-switcher sync fleksi" successfully on P17. We are on fleksi now.  janfr@Fleksi:~$ pc-switcher sync p17 --dry-run bash: /home/janfr/.local/bin/pc-switcher: cannot execute: required file not f…

aiTitle: Fix pc-switcher sync command not found error

content.text: `cannot execute: required file not found` on an existing file is a broken shebang — the interpreter path in the script's first line doesn't exist. Since you just synced `/home` from P17, the `pc-switcher` launcher (and likely its venv) got overwritten with P17's copy, whose shebang points at an interpreter path that doesn't exist on fleksi. Let me confirm.

file.content:
# Group 1: size token (e.g. "9.53G" or "29,958,458") — used to compute
# bytes_transferred (WR-01).  rsync thousands-groups the plain byte counter with
# a locale-dependent separator (',' on C/en_US, '.' on nl_BE-style), so the size
# class must accept both: dropping ',' made re.search() stop at the first comma
# and capture only the last 1-3 digits (e.g. "29,958,458" -> "458"), skewing
# bytes_transferred by orders of magnitude while files-transferred (ungrouped)
# stayed correct.
# Group 2: percent complete.  Group 3: files transferred so far.  Group 4: total-to-check.
_PROGRESS2_RE = re.compile(r"(\d+[\d,.]*[KMGT]?)\s+(\d+)%\s+\S+\s+\S+\s+\(xfr#(\d+),\s*to-chk=\d+/(\d+)\)")

# pc-switcher's own runtime state and installation live under the invoking
# user's home directory. Mirroring them between machines is unsafe:
# sync-history.json is the topology-safety state (ADR-015) and a --delete mirror
# would clobber the target's copy mid-sync; the lock file, logs, and the running
# install must stay machine-local. These paths are ALWAYS excluded from folder
# sync regardless of user config, and are the ONLY hardcoded excludes — every
# other exclusion is user-configurable (ADR-016).
_RUNTIME_EXCLUDE_RELPATHS: tuple[str, ...] = (
    ".local/share/pc-switcher",  # lock, sync-history.json, logs/
    ".local/share/uv/tools/pcswitcher",  # uv tool install (virtualenv)
    ".local/bin/pc-switcher",  # entry-point shim
)


@dataclass
class FolderEntry:
    """A single folder to sync, with its optional central rsync filter file.

    `filter_file` is a per-folder central rsync merge filter file (native
    rsync `+`/`-` filter syntax, first-match-wins); unset/empty means no
    central merge rule for that folder (runtime excludes and the tree-wide
    `dir-merge /.pcswitcher-filter` still apply).
    """

    path: str
    enabled: bool = True
    filter_file: str | None = None

    def expanded_filter_file(self) -> str | None:
        """Return the ~- and env-var-expanded absolute filter_file path, or None if unset.

        Expansion uses the invoking user's environment because the source is
        always the local machine and rsync reads the merge file locally there
        (the merge/dir-merge rules only take effect on the source side of the
        transfer; see `_build_rsync_cmd`).

content.content:
# Python Development Conventions

Apply to Python code/projects. New or owner-controlled projects: use defaults. Existing: follow project config (Python version, deps, tests, lint, CI) unless explicitly migrating. Use modern standards for supported version.

## Python Version and Modern Practices

- New Python projects: target >=3.14
- Existing projects: use modern syntax for configured version
- Use modern type syntax: e.g. `str | None` instead of `Optional[str]`, `list[str]` instead of `List[str]`
- Include `from __future__ import annotations` when improves annotation behavior for Python version
- Use `@override` for method overrides when available in Python version
- Prefer `StrEnum` for string-based enums when available in Python version
- Prefer `Path` from pathlib for local filesystem paths

## Project Setup and Developer Workflows

- MUST use uv for running code, managing dependencies in new projects
- Existing projects: use project's workflow. Don't replace Poetry/Hatch/tox/Makefiles unless asked
- Use plain uv commands: `uv sync`, `uv run ...`, `uv add`, etc. Avoid `uv pip`. NEVER use `uv pip install` unless project intentionally uses uv's pip-compatible interface
- Use `codespell` for checking typos in code/comments
- Use `ruff` for linting, formatting
- Use `basedpyright` for type checking
- Use `pytest` for testing

## Code Quality Standards

- Full type annotations for function signatures, modern syntax per Python version
- Import types from correct modules (e.g., use `collections.abc` not `typing` for collection types)
- Proper error handling with specific exception types, meaningful messages
- Single responsibility principle, keep functions focused
- Use context managers for resource management
- Prefer composition over inheritance
- Self-documenting code with meaningful names
- Follow PEP 8 (max 119 chars), use Python strengths, avoid anti-patterns

## Documentation Philosophy

- Document architectural decisions, design patterns
- Concise docstrings: focus on "why" not obvious "what"
- Explain complex logic rationales inline when needed
- Use backticks for variable/parameter names in docstrings
- Avoid repetitive or obvious comments

## Testing Strategy

- Small focused tests, clear locality. Use repo's layout (colocated, `tests/` tree, doctests)
- Avoid trivial tests that test language itself
- Simple assertions over complex pytest fixtures when possible
- Test edge cases, errors, boundary values
- Mock external dependencies, I/O operations appropriately
- Mocked integration behavior: add real-dep integration coverage where practical
- Never use `assert False` - raise `AssertionError` with explanation instead

## Performance Optimization Techniques

- Use appropriate data structures (sets for membership, deque for queues, etc.)
- Implement generators for memory-efficient iteration over large datasets
- Apply caching strategies where beneficial
- Utilize built-in functions/libraries (itertools, collections, functools)
- Consider algorithmic complexity, choose efficient algorithms
- Profile code using cProfile, line_profiler, or memory_profiler before optimizing

## Error Handling and Type Checking

- Use `# pyright: ignore` sparingly, always with explanation
- Raise `AssertionError` with descriptive messages for invariant violations
- Handle exceptions at appropriate levels, avoid bare except
- Use specific exception types that convey meaning

## File Operations Best Practices

- Prefer `Path` objects for local filesystem paths
- Atomic file writing for user data, config, state or other where partial output harmful
- Use context managers for file operations
- Handle encoding explicitly (default UTF-8)

## Core Principles

- Pragmatic: avoid over-engineering, premature optimization
- Leverage stdlib first. Use third-party packages sparingly
- Code clarity over clever tricks
- Aim for functional, clean, maintainable, performant code

Apply to Python code/projects. New or owner-controlled projects: use defaults. Existing: follow project config (Python version, deps, tests, lint, CI) unless explicitly migrating. Use modern standards for supported version.

## Python Version and Modern Practices

- New Python projects: target >=3.14
- Existing projects: use modern syntax for configured version
- Use modern type syntax: e.g. `str | None` instead of `Optional[str]`, `list[str]` instead of `List[str]`
- Include `from __future__ import annotations` when improves annotation behavior for Python version
- Use `@override` for method overrides when available in Python version
- Prefer `StrEnum` for string-based enums when available in Python version
- Prefer `Path` from pathlib for local filesystem paths

## Project Setup and Developer Workflows

- MUST use uv for running code, managing dependencies in new projects
- Existing projects: use project's workflow. Don't replace Poetry/Hatch/tox/Makefiles unless asked
- Use plain uv commands: `uv sync`, `uv run ...`, `uv add`, etc. Avoid `uv pip`. NEVER use `uv pip install` unless project intentionally uses uv's pip-compatible interface
- Use `codespell` for checking typos in code/comments
- Use `ruff` for linting, formatting
- Use `basedpyright` for type checking
- Use `pytest` for testing

## Code Quality Standards

- Full type annotations for function signatures, modern syntax per Python version
- Import types from correct modules (e.g., use `collections.abc` not `typing` for collection types)
- Proper error handling with specific exception types, meaningful messages
- Single responsibility principle, keep functions focused
- Use context managers for resource management
- Prefer composition over inheritance
- Self-documenting code with meaningful names
- Follow PEP 8 (max 119 chars), use Python strengths, avoid anti-patterns

## Documentation Philosophy

- Document architectural decisions, design patterns
- Concise docstrings: focus on "why" not obvious "what"
- Explain complex logic rationales inline when needed
- Use backticks for variable/parameter names in docstrings
- Avoid repetitive or obvious comments

## Testing Strategy

- Small focused tests, clear locality. Use repo's layout (colocated, `tests/` tree, doctests)
- Avoid trivial tests that test language itself
- Simple assertions over complex pytest fixtures when possible
- Test edge cases, errors, boundary values
- Mock external dependencies, I/O operations appropriately
- Mocked integration behavior: add real-dep integration coverage where practical
- Never use `assert False` - raise `AssertionError` with explanation instead

## Performance Optimization Techniques

- Use appropriate data structures (sets for membership, deque for queues, etc.)
- Implement generators for memory-efficient iteration over large datasets
- Apply caching strategies where beneficial
- Utilize built-in functions/libraries (itertools, collections, functools)
- Consider algorithmic complexity, choose efficient algorithms
- Profile code using cProfile, line_profiler, or memory_profiler before optimizing

## Error Handling and Type Checking

- Use `# pyright: ignore` sparingly, always with explanation
- Raise `AssertionError` with descriptive messages for invariant violations
- Handle exceptions at appropriate levels, avoid bare except
- Use specific exception types that convey meaning

## File Operations Best Practices

- Prefer `Path` objects for local filesystem paths
- Atomic file writing for user data, config, state or other where partial output harmful
- Use context managers for file operations
- Handle encoding explicitly (default UTF-8)

## Core Principles

- Pragmatic: avoid over-engineering, premature optimization
- Leverage stdlib first. Use third-party packages sparingly
- Code clarity over clever tricks
- Aim for functional, clean, maintainable, performant code

content.content:
# Python Development Commands

New or owner-controlled projects → uv. Existing repos → follow project's setup unless asked.

## Package Management with uv

Prefer uv (new projects). Skip `uv pip` unless project uses it. Don't replace other package managers without ask.

```bash
# Initialize new project
uv init

# Add dependencies
uv add package_name
uv add --dev ipykernel pytest ruff basedpyright  # dev dependencies

# Sync dependencies
uv sync

# Show installed packages
uv tree
uv tree --package package_name

# Run commands in virtual environment
uv run python script.py
uv run pytest
```

## Testing with pytest

```bash
# Run all tests
uv run pytest

# Run tests with coverage
uv run pytest --cov=src --cov-report=term-missing

# Run specific test file or function
uv run pytest tests/test_module.py
uv run pytest tests/test_module.py::test_function

# Run tests matching pattern
uv run pytest -k "pattern"

# Run tests with verbose output
uv run pytest -v

# Run tests and stop on first failure
uv run pytest -x
```

## Linting and Formatting with ruff

```bash
# Format code
uv run ruff format .

# Check linting issues
uv run ruff check .

# Fix auto-fixable issues
uv run ruff check --fix .

# Check specific file
uv run ruff check path/to/file.py
```

## Type Checking with basedpyright

```bash
# Type check entire project
uv run basedpyright

# Type check specific file
uv run basedpyright path/to/file.py
```

## Code Quality Checks

```bash
# Run all quality checks (typical CI pipeline)
uv run ruff format --check .
uv run ruff check .
uv run basedpyright
uv run pytest

# Check for common typos
uv run codespell
```

## Profiling and Performance

```bash
# Profile with cProfile
uv run python -m cProfile -s cumtime script.py

# Profile with line_profiler
uv run kernprof -l -v script.py

# Memory profiling
uv run python -m memory_profiler script.py
```

## Common Development Patterns

```bash
# Check outdated packages
uv tree --outdated

# Clean pyc files and caches only when explicitly requested
find . -type d -name "__pycache__" -exec rm -rf {} +
find . -type d -name ".pytest_cache" -exec rm -rf {} +
find . -type d -name ".ruff_cache" -exec rm -rf {} +
find . -type f -name "*.pyc" -delete
```

"""Installation job for ensuring pc-switcher is installed on target."""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, ClassVar

from pcswitcher.install import get_install_with_script_command_line
from pcswitcher.jobs.base import SystemJob
from pcswitcher.jobs.context import JobContext
from pcswitcher.models import CommandResult, Host, LogLevel
from pcswitcher.version import Release, Version, find_one_version, get_this_version

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from pcswitcher.models import ValidationError


class InstallOnTargetJob(SystemJob):
    """Ensure pc-switcher is installed on target machine.

    This job verifies version compatibility and installs or upgrades pc-switcher
    if needed. Version validation occurs during the validate phase to detect
    incompatibilities early. It runs AFTER pre-sync snapshots to enable rollback
    if installation fails.
    """

    name: ClassVar[str] = "install_on_target"

    def __init__(self, context: JobContext) -> None:
        """Initialize install job with context.

        Args:
            context: JobContext with executors, config, and event bus
        """
        super().__init__(context)
        self.source_version: Version = get_this_version()
        self.target_version: Version | None = None

    async def validate(self) -> list[ValidationError]:
        """Validate version compatibility between source and target.

        Checks that target pc-switcher version is not newer than source,
        which would indicate a version conflict that cannot be resolved.

        Returns:
            List of ValidationError if version check fails, empty list otherwise
        """
        # Check target version (login_shell ensures PATH includes ~/.local/bin and GITHUB_TOKEN is available)
        result = await self.target.run_command("pc-switcher --version 2>/dev/null", login_shell=True)
        if result.success:
            # Parse version string from output (e.g., "pc-switcher 0.4.0" -> "0.4.0")
            self.target_version = find_one_version(result.stdout)
            if self.target_version > self.source_version:
                return [
                    self._validation_error(
                        Host.TARGET,
                        f"Target version {self.target_version} is newer than source {self.source_version}",
                    )
                ]

        return []

    async def _run_install(self, v: Release | Version | str | None = None, /) -> CommandResult:
        """Run install script on target."""
        cmd = get_install_with_script_command_line(v)
        return await self.target.run_command(cmd, login_shell=False)

    async def execute(self) -> None:
        """Install or upgrade pc-switcher on target if needed."""
        # Check target version (already validated in validate phase)
        if self.target_version:
            if self.target_version == self.source_version:
                self._log(
                    Host.TARGET,
                    LogLevel.INFO,
                    f"Target pc-switcher version matches source: {self.source_version}, no install needed",
                )
                return
            self._log(
                Host.TARGET,
                LogLevel.INFO,
                f"Upgrading pc-switcher on target from {self.target_version} to {self.source_version}",
            )
        else:
            self._log(
                Host.TARGET,
                LogLevel.INFO,
                f"Installing pc-switcher {self.source_version} on target",
            )

        # In dry-run mode, skip actual installation
        if self.context.dry_run:
            return

        # Run the same install.sh script used for initial installation
        # The script handles: uv bootstrap, dependencies, pc-switcher install

        # To minimize github lookups, we first try to install directly using the exact source version.
        # If that fails, we fall back to the release floor, which needs a GitHub API call to lookup the releases.
        result = await self._run_install(self.source_version)
        source_release = None
        if result.success:
            installed_version_str = f"v{self.source_version}"
        else:
            # Get the GitHub floor release for this version (the highest release <= this version).
            # For dev versions like 0.1.0a3.post23.dev0, this returns the base release (e.g., 0.1.0a3).
            source_release = self.source_version.get_release_floor()
            # Expected path for a released source version: the exact-version attempt
            # targets a PEP440 tag (e.g. 0.1.0a14) that never matches the semver release
            # tag (v0.1.0-alpha.14), so it falls through to the release floor. This is
            # normal, not an error — log at DEBUG (file only). A genuine failure surfaces
            # via the RuntimeError below when the fallback itself fails.
            self._log(
                Host.TARGET,
                LogLevel.DEBUG,
                f"Exact-version install (v{self.source_version}) not available as a tag; "
                f"installing via release {source_release.tag}",
            )
            result = await self._run_install(source_release)
            if not result.success:
                raise RuntimeError(f"Failed to install pc-switcher on target: {result.stderr}")
            installed_version_str = source_release.tag

        # Verify installation (login_shell ensures PATH includes ~/.local/bin)
        result = await self.target.run_command("pc-switcher --version", login_shell=True)
        if not result.success:
            raise RuntimeError("Installation verification failed: pc-switcher not found")
        # Parse and compare versions properly (handles both PEP440 and SemVer formats)
        installed_version = find_one_version(result.stdout)
        if installed_version != (source_release or self.source_version):
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"Installation verification failed: expected {installed_version_str}, got v{installed_version}"
            )

        self._log(
            Host.TARGET,
            LogLevel.INFO,
            f"Target pc-switcher installed/upgraded to {installed_version_str}",
        )

content.text:
Install-on-target doesn't copy files — it runs the install *script* (`uv tool install` via install.sh) on fleksi over SSH, then verifies `pc-switcher --version` succeeds (lines 124-126). So right after that job, pc-switcher *did* run on fleksi. The breakage happened elsewhere. 

I need to see (a) how the runtime excludes are anchored into the rsync command, (b) whether folder sync uses `--delete`, and (c) what the actual sync config syncs. Let me pull all three, plus note: I already reinstalled, which overwrote the original venv evidence — so I'll rely on code + config + logs.

file.content:
def _runtime_exclude_filters(folder_path: str) -> list[str]:
        """rsync `--filter` args protecting pc-switcher's own runtime files.

        The runtime files (`_RUNTIME_EXCLUDE_RELPATHS`) live under the invoking
        user's home directory. When `folder_path` contains that home (e.g. syncing
        `/home`), each protected path is emitted as a root-anchored rsync pattern
        relative to the transfer root, so it matches exactly one location and can
        never be re-exposed by a user include rule. When home is outside
        `folder_path` (e.g. `/root` synced by a normal user) there is nothing to
        protect and the list is empty.
        """
        try:
            rel = Path.home().relative_to(folder_path.rstrip("/") or "/")
        except ValueError:
            return []
        prefix = "/" if str(rel) == "." else f"/{rel}/"
        return [f"--filter={shlex.quote(f'- {prefix}{sub}')}" for sub in _RUNTIME_EXCLUDE_RELPATHS]

    def _transport_args(self) -> list[str]:
        """Build the rsync `-e <ssh>` transport and `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` args.

        Shared by the main mirror (`_build_rsync_cmd`) and the filter pre-seed pass
        (`_build_preseed_cmd`) so both use identical SSH credentials and target-side
        sudo elevation.

        SSH transport note: `sudo -E rsync` runs rsync as root, and the ssh it spawns
        also runs as root.  OpenSSH resolves `~/.ssh` from the running uid's passwd
        entry (root → /root/.ssh) regardless of the $HOME variable.  Passing explicit
        credentials (-l, -i, -o UserKnownHostsFile=, -F) with the invoking user's paths
        is the correct fix; relying on $HOME does NOT work (empirically verified on
        target VMs: ssh fails with "Host key verification failed" / rsync exit 255
        without explicit credentials).
        """
        ssh_dir = Path.home() / ".ssh"
        target_user = self.context.target_username or getpass.getuser()

        ssh_tokens: list[str] = ["ssh", "-T", "-q", "-l", target_user]

        # -F before host-name flags so it takes effect for all connection params.
        config_file = ssh_dir / "config"
        if config_file.exists():
            ssh_tokens += ["-F", str(config_file)]

        known_hosts_file = ssh_dir / "known_hosts"
        if known_hosts_file.exists():
            ssh_tokens += ["-o", f"UserKnownHostsFile={known_hosts_file}"]

        # Offer all default key types that exist; ssh tries each in order.
        # SSH_AUTH_SOCK (preserved by sudo -E) is also available when an agent
        # is running, but explicit -i keys cover the no-agent case.
        for key_name in ("id_ed25519", "id_ecdsa", "id_rsa"):
            key_file = ssh_dir / key_name
            if key_file.exists():
                ssh_tokens += ["-i", str(key_file)]

        # shlex.join quotes individual tokens (handles spaces in paths).
        # shlex.quote wraps the assembled command for the outer -e argument.
        ssh_cmd = shlex.join(ssh_tokens)
        # Root on target via passwordless sudo scoped to /usr/bin/rsync (D-05).
        return [f"-e {shlex.quote(ssh_cmd)}", f"--rsync-path={shlex.quote('sudo rsync')}"]

    async def _needs_seeding_pass(self, folder: FolderEntry) -> bool:
        """Whether a no-delete seeding pass must run before the deleting mirror.

        A dir-merge rule is read from the *receiver* during the delete scan, so the
        deleting mirror protects the target correctly only when every source
        `.pcswitcher-filter` is already present and byte-identical on the target.  In
        the steady state that already holds — filter files sync like any other file —
        so a single pass is safe and the whole tree is walked only once.  A seeding
        pass is needed only when a per-directory filter was added or changed on the

# PC-switcher configuration file
# Location: ~/.config/pc-switcher/config.yaml
# Documentation: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher
# Full config reference: https://github.com/flaksit/pc-switcher/blob/main/docs/configuration.md

# =============================================================================
# Logging Configuration (3-setting model)
# =============================================================================
# Controls where log messages appear based on their severity level.
#
# Log levels (from most to least verbose):
#   DEBUG (10)    - Internal diagnostics, very verbose
#   FULL (15)     - Operational details (file-level sync info)
#   INFO (20)     - High-level progress and status updates
#   WARNING (30)  - Unexpected but non-fatal conditions
#   ERROR (40)    - Recoverable errors
#   CRITICAL (50) - Unrecoverable errors, sync must abort

logging:
  # file: Floor level for log FILE output (~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/)
  #       Messages at or above this level are written to the log file.
  file: DEBUG

  # tui: Floor level for TERMINAL output during sync
  #      Messages at or above this level are shown in the terminal UI.
  tui: INFO

  # external: Additional floor for THIRD-PARTY libraries (asyncssh, etc.)
  #           External library logs must pass BOTH this floor AND the
  #           destination floor (file or tui) to appear in output.
  #           Use DEBUG to see SSH connection details, WARNING to suppress noise.
  external: WARNING

# =============================================================================
# Sync Jobs Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Enable or disable optional sync jobs
# Note: Only jobs explicitly listed here will be discovered and run
# Set to false to disable a job (e.g., dummy_fail: false)

sync_jobs:
  # Test jobs (for development/testing)
  dummy_success: false   # Test job that completes successfully
  dummy_fail: false      # Test job that fails at configurable progress

  folder_sync: true          # Sync /home and /root directories (Phase 1)

  # Future jobs (uncomment when available):
  # packages: true        # Sync apt, snap, flatpak packages
  # docker: false         # Sync Docker images, containers, volumes
  # vms: false            # Sync KVM/virt-manager VMs
  # k3s: false            # Sync k3s cluster state

# =============================================================================
# Disk Space Monitoring
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for disk space checks before and during sync
# Threshold format: percentage (e.g., "20%") or absolute (e.g., "50GiB")

disk_space_monitor:
  # Minimum free space required before sync starts
  preflight_minimum: "20%"

  # Minimum free space required during sync (CRITICAL - abort if below)
  runtime_minimum: "15%"

  # Free space threshold for warnings during sync
  warning_threshold: "25%"

  # Seconds between disk space checks during sync
  check_interval: 30

# =============================================================================
# Btrfs Snapshot Management
# =============================================================================
# Configuration for automatic btrfs snapshots before and after sync
# IMPORTANT: Subvolumes must match YOUR system's btrfs layout

btrfs_snapshots:
  # List of btrfs subvolumes to snapshot
  # Check your system: sudo btrfs subvolume list /
  # Common layouts:
  #   - Ubuntu default: ["@", "@home"]
  #   - Single root: ["@"]
  subvolumes:
    - "@"
    - "@home"

  # Number of recent sync sessions to always retain snapshots for,
  # irrespective of age (max_age_days below)
  keep_recent: 3

  # Maximum age in days for snapshot retention (optional)
  # Note that always keeping 'keep_recent' snapshots takes precedence.
  # Uncomment to enable age-based cleanup:
  # max_age_days: 7

# =============================================================================
# Job-Specific Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Each sync job can have its own configuration section
# Configuration keys must match the job name

# Test job configurations (examples)
dummy_success:
  source_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 20    # Seconds to run on target machine

dummy_fail:
  source_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on source machine
  target_duration: 10    # Seconds to run on target machine
  fail_at: 12            # Elapsed seconds at which to fail

# =============================================================================
# Folder Sync Configuration
# =============================================================================
# Defines folders to sync via rsync. Each entry mirrors `path` (rsync --delete)
# minus the paths matched by its filter rules. Two authoring surfaces are
# available, both native rsync filter syntax (`+` include, `-` exclude,
# first-match-wins):
#   - filter_file: a per-folder central filter, spliced via rsync `merge`.
#     Optional; omit it for a folder that needs no central rule list (the
#     runtime excludes and any .pcswitcher-filter files still apply).
#   - .pcswitcher-filter: a tree-wide per-directory file, spliced via rsync
#     `dir-merge`; it inherits into subdirectories and can re-include a
#     subtree a broader exclude would otherwise drop (e.g. keep ~/.cache/uv
#     while dropping the rest of ~/.cache).
# GLOBAL-FIRST precedence: the central filter_file is evaluated before any
# .pcswitcher-filter, so central policy always wins. pc-switcher's own runtime
# files (ADR-016) are excluded first of all and can never be re-exposed by
# either surface.
#
# ~/.ssh/authorized_keys: the shipped home.filter/root.filter EXCLUDE it, so
# each machine keeps its own SSH access list. pc-switcher connects as the normal
# user, so the target authorizes the sync against that user's authorized_keys;
# mirroring a copy that omits the source machine's own key would delete the key
# the next sync needs and lock the sync out of its own target. To sync it anyway,
# make it one shared fleet list containing EVERY machine's public key (its own
# included), then drop the exclude line. See docs/configuration.md
# "authorized_keys".
#
# See docs/configuration.md for the full filter-rule syntax and a guide for
# users coming from .gitignore.

folder_sync:
  folders:
    - path: /home/janfr
      enabled: true
      filter_file: ~/.config/pc-switcher/home.filter
global_settings:
  log_file_level: FULL
  log_cli_level: INFO
  sync_jobs:
    user_data: true
    packages: true

btrfs_snapshots:
  subvolumes:
    - "@"
    - "@home"

# pc-switcher central filter file for the /home/janfr folder_sync entry.
# Native rsync filter syntax: `+` includes, `-` excludes, first-match-wins.
# The transfer root is /home/janfr's contents, so a leading `/` anchors to the
# top level of this home dir (e.g. `- /.ssh/id_*` == /home/janfr/.ssh/id_*).
# IMPORTANT: rsync does NOT strip trailing `# comments` from a rule line — the
# whole rest of the line (spaces included) becomes the pattern. So every comment
# lives on its OWN line; never append one after a `- pattern`.
# See docs/configuration.md "Filter rules" and "Coming from .gitignore".

# --- Secrets: kept machine-specific ---
# SSH private keys are per-machine auth identity — never copied between machines.
- /.ssh/id_*
# NOTE: .ssh/authorized_keys IS synced here (intentionally not excluded). This is
# a shared fleet access list: it lists EVERY pc-switcher machine's public key,
# including this machine's own, so a mirror never drops the key the next sync's
# SSH connection depends on. Keep every machine's key in it or a sync will lock
# itself out of its own target. See docs/configuration.md "authorized_keys".
# NOTE: .ssh/known_hosts IS synced — it holds only PUBLIC host keys of servers
# you connect to (not a secret), and having the same trusted hosts on both
# laptops is convenient. Only caveat: if the two laptops ever SSH into each
# other, each will re-prompt once for the other's (differing) host key.

# GNOME login keyring is encrypted with your login password/TPM; the blob
# would not decrypt on the other machine, so it is useless there.
- /.local/share/keyrings

# NOTE: .gnupg and .password-store ARE synced — your GPG identity and the pass
# store (itself GPG-encrypted) are user identity you want on both machines;
# pass is useless without its GPG key, so they travel together.

# --- ~/.cache: allowlist — keep relocatable download caches, drop the rest ---
# .cache holds only regenerable caches, so DROP all of it except a few download
# caches that re-fetch expensively and reuse cleanly on another same-arch machine:
#   uv            — 6.7G package cache; `uv cache prune` keeps it bounded
#   pip           — wheel/download cache
#   ms-playwright — version-pinned e2e browser binaries (~650M); no auto-GC, trim
#                   with `playwright uninstall --all` + reinstall
# `/***` (not `/**`) re-includes each kept dir ENTRY so the trailing `- /.cache/*`
# can't drop it and rsync descends into it. Add `+ /.cache/<tool>/***` above the
# `- /.cache/*` line to keep another cache.
# Dropped & rebuilt on target: nvidia/mesa shader caches, fontconfig, tracker3,
# thumbnails, gnome-software, *.kcache, browser/app caches (chrome, spotify,
# electron, ...), pre-commit venvs, node-gyp headers, ms-playwright-go, etc.
# NOTE: npm (~/.npm) and bun (~/.bun) caches live OUTSIDE .cache and mostly stay
# synced (relocatable download caches). Exception: ~/.npm/_npx below.
+ /.cache/
+ /.cache/uv/***
+ /.cache/pip/***
+ /.cache/ms-playwright/***
- /.cache/*

# --- Tool caches outside .cache ---
# kubectl discovery/http cache — regenerated on use.
- /.kube/cache

# --- ~/.npm: keep _cacache (download cache), drop _npx ---
# _npx is npx's per-spec materialized installs (MCP servers, etc.). It re-links
# from _cacache with no network fetch, so dropping it costs only a quick offline
# rebuild on first use — and it accumulates one-off / old-version junk. _cacache
# (the actual tarball cache) stays synced.
- /.npm/_npx

# --- Machine/hardware-specific (outside .cache): wrong/useless on other HW ---
# NVIDIA compute cache.
- /.nv
# GNOME display layout — this machine's monitors.
- /.config/monitors.xml
- /.config/monitors.xml~
# KDE screen/output config — this machine's displays.
- /.local/share/kscreen
# KDE file index (~838M) — rebuilt on target.
- /.local/share/baloo
# Per-machine hardware fingerprint — never copy identity.
- /.hwid
# nvidia-settings GUI config — this machine's GPU/displays.
- /.nvidia-settings-rc

# --- App caches under .config / .var (not under .cache) ---
- /.config/Code/Cache
- /.config/Code/CachedData
- /.config/Code/GPUCache
- /.config/Code/Code Cache
- /.config/Code/CachedExtensionVSIXs
- /.config/Claude/Cache
- /.config/Claude/Code Cache
- /.config/Claude/GPUCache
- /.config/Claude/DawnWebGPUCache
- /.config/Claude/DawnGraphiteCache
# flatpak per-app XDG cache (Firefox ~1.2G, etc.).
- /.var/app/*/cache
# Electron flatpak apps bury caches under config/<App>/ (missed by */cache above);
# Slack alone is ~1.85G here. All regenerable.
- /.var/app/*/config/*/Cache
- /.var/app/*/config/*/Code Cache
- /.var/app/*/config/*/GPUCache
- /.var/app/*/config/*/Service Worker

# --- Large machine-local regenerable, no value on the other machine ---
# Claude Desktop's Cowork / local-agent VM (~30G; the .cowork-adopted marker
# names it). rootfs.img is a materialization of a versioned base (re-hydrated
# from rootfs.img.zst or re-downloaded); sessiondata.img is the only truly
# local state, and only matters if you run Cowork sessions. Not using Cowork,
# so nothing is lost and no download results from excluding it — Desktop only
# fetches the VM when you actually start a local-agent session. If you adopt
# Cowork and want it offline, pre-sync this over LAN with Claude Desktop CLOSED
# (VM stopped => consistent copy; only mid-session syncing risks a torn image).
- /.config/Claude/vm_bundles

# Trash can.
- /.local/share/Trash

# --- Flatpak app binaries: OSTree content store ---
# Must be provisioned with `flatpak install`, not rsynced (hardlinked,
# content-addressed; rsync can corrupt/desync it). App DATA under .var/app
# IS synced, so once the app is installed on the target its settings/state
# are already there. (A future pc-switcher packages job will install these.)
- /.local/share/flatpak

# --- Device-linked app: won't work when copied ---
# Signal Desktop is linked per-device; copying its data breaks the link.
- /.var/app/org.signal.Signal

# --- Cloud-synced folders: each machine runs its own daemon ---
# Only the machine-STATE and the CONTENT are excluded, NOT the client binaries.
# Copying the daemon STATE would make Dropbox/Koofr think the travel laptop
# is this same install (shared host id / file DB) -> unlink, re-index, or
# conflicts. Copying the CONTENT folders on top of an independently-running
# daemon causes double-sync and "conflicted copy" files. So each machine
# links its own daemon (log in once on the target) and the content is left
# to those daemons entirely.
# NOTE: .dropbox-dist and .koofr-dist (the client binaries) ARE synced.
# Koofr has no package-manager install — ~/.koofr-dist IS the whole app
# (autostart runs /home/janfr/.koofr-dist/storagegui), so excluding it would
# leave Koofr missing on the target. Dropbox's daemon self-re-downloads, but
# syncing it (same arch) avoids a ~150M fetch on the road.
# Dropbox daemon state (host id, file DB) — per machine.
- /.dropbox
# Dropbox content — handled by the Dropbox daemon.
- /Dropbox
# Koofr daemon state + config.json (per-machine link/selective-sync).
- /.koofr
# Koofr content — handled by the Koofr daemon.
- /Koofr

# --- Session runtime junk ---
# X session log for this boot.
- /.xsession-errors

self._ui.set_current_step(4, "Discover jobs")

            # Phase 5: Disk space preflight check
            await self._check_disk_space_preflight()
            self._ui.set_current_step(5, "Disk check")

            # Phase 6: Pre-sync snapshots
            self._logger.info("Creating pre-sync snapshots", extra={"job": "orchestrator", "host": "source"})
            await self._create_snapshots(SnapshotPhase.PRE)
            self._ui.set_current_step(6, "Pre-sync snapshots")

            # Phase 7: Install/upgrade pc-switcher on target (after snapshots for rollback safety)
            self._logger.info(
                "Ensuring pc-switcher is installed on target",
                extra={"job": "orchestrator", "host": "target"},
            )
            await self._install_on_target_job()
            self._ui.set_current_step(7, "Install on target")

            # Phase 8: Sync config from source to target
            self._logger.info("Syncing configuration to target", extra={"job": "orchestrator", "host": "target"})
            await self._sync_config_to_target()
            self._ui.set_current_step(8, "Sync config")

            # Phase 9: Execute sync jobs with background monitoring
            self._logger.info("Starting sync operations", extra={"job": "orchestrator", "host": "source"})
            job_results = await self._execute_jobs(jobs)
            session.job_results = job_results

            # Phase 10: Post-sync snapshots

aiTitle: Fix pc-switcher sync command not found error

content.text:
Confirmed the full mechanism from code + config. You were right to push back — the reinstall treats the symptom and the *next* sync from P17 can re-break it the same way.

## What actually happened

The synced folder is `/home/janfr`, mirrored with `rsync --delete` through `home.filter`. Two facts collide:

1. **The tool venv is protected, but its interpreter is not.** The runtime excludes (`folder_sync.py:47-51`) protect only `.local/share/uv/tools/pcswitcher` (the venv) and the `.local/bin/pc-switcher` shim. They do **not** protect `.local/share/uv/python/` — the actual interpreter builds. And `home.filter` has no rule for `.local/share/uv/` either. So `.local/share/uv/python/` is a full `--delete` mirror of P17's copy.

2. **uv interpreters differ per machine by patch version.** Each machine downloads whatever was the latest 3.14 patch *when it first needed one*. Fleksi's venv was built (by an earlier `uv tool install` run *on fleksi*) against `cpython-3.14.6`, which uv downloaded to fleksi at that time. P17 never had 3.14.6 — it has 3.14.0/3.14.3.

So when folder_sync runs, its `--delete` mirror forces fleksi's `.local/share/uv/python/` to *exactly match P17's set*: it copies P17's 3.14.0/3.14.3 in, and **deletes fleksi's 3.14.6** because the source doesn't have it. The venv (frozen, protected) keeps pointing at the now-deleted `cpython-3.14.6/bin/python3.14`. Dangling symlink → the shebang interpreter doesn't exist → `cannot execute: required file not found`.

This is self-consistent: the venv was *not* copied from P17 (the exclude works — otherwise P17's 3.14.6 would have come along with it and still be here). It was fleksi-local, and the only thing that deletes files in that tree is the `--delete` mirror.

## Answering your two questions directly

**"Why didn't the matching interpreter build sync?"** — It can't. The mirror makes fleksi match *the source*, and P17 doesn't have 3.14.6. The sync didn't fail to *add* the right interpreter; it actively *deleted* the one the venv needed, because that interpreter exists only on fleksi. Mirroring per-machine uv interpreters with `--delete` is fundamentally wrong — it deletes the target's in-use interpreters.

**"How can `uv tool install` succeed yet pc-switcher not run?"** — Ordering. Install-on-target is **Phase 7**; folder_sync is **Phase 9** (`orchestrator.py:361` vs `371`). Install-on-target really did run `uv tool install` on fleksi and verified `pc-switcher --version` (`install_on_target.py:124-126`) — it genuinely worked at that moment. Then Phase 9's `--delete` mirror deleted the interpreter that freshly-built venv depends on, silently re-breaking it *within the same sync*. The success was real but transient. (If instead the pre-sync venv already worked and versions matched, install-on-target was *skipped* — same end result: Phase 9 deletes the interpreter either way.)

## The design flaw

Protecting the tool venv while `--delete`-mirroring the interpreter tree it depends on is incoherent. Either both are machine-local or neither is. Root causes to fix, not the symlink:

- `.local/share/uv/python/` (uv-managed interpreters) should be excluded from the mirror — they're machine-local, per-machine patch versions, and re-provisioned by uv on demand. Same likely applies to `.local/share/uv/tools/` generally (every tool venv there has the same latent breakage, not just pcswitcher).

I haven't verified P17's interpreter set directly (I'm on fleksi, no access) — that one link is inferred from the mirror logic + fleksi's current state, though it's forced by the evidence. Everything else is read from the code/config.

Want me to (a) file this as a bug, (b) draft the filter fix for `.local/share/uv/`, or just leave it here? I won't change anything until you say.

message.content:
actually, maybe install_on_target was not a good idea. If we would just let foldersync sync .local/share/uv/tools/pcswitcher, there wouldn't have been any problem at all. (But still need to install uv if that isn't present on the target - even though to following "sync packages" feature will take care of that, too.)
What do you think?

content.text: Good instinct — let me pressure-test it against the code before I opine, because the answer hinges on one thing: does anything actually *execute* `pc-switcher` (or `uv`) on the target *during* a sync? If not, your simplification is clean. If yes, a venv that only arrives in Phase 9 is too late.

aiTitle: Fix pc-switcher sync command not found error

content.text:
Verified the load-bearing fact: **nothing executes `pc-switcher` or `uv` on the target during a sync except `install_on_target` itself.** Every other target-side command is a primitive — `btrfs`, `rsync`, `df`, `dpkg`, `mkdir`, `cat`, `systemctl`. The sync is fully source-orchestrated. So `install_on_target`'s only real job is provisioning the tool on the target *for future use* (so you can later sync *from* it), not for the sync that's running.

That means your instinct is right, and it's more than a bugfix — it's a genuine simplification. My take:

## Why it works

A uv tool venv is self-contained and needs no `uv` at runtime: `pc-switcher` is just `shim → venv/bin/pc-switcher → shebang python`. All three live under `/home/janfr`:

- `.local/bin/pc-switcher` (shim) — currently runtime-excluded
- `.local/share/uv/tools/pcswitcher` (venv) — currently runtime-excluded
- `.local/share/uv/python/cpython-*` (interpreter) — **already mirrored** (this is what the bug was about)
- `.local/bin/uv` (the uv binary itself) — **already mirrored** too

So the fix is the inverse of what exists: stop *protecting* the venv+shim and let all four ride the same rsync pass. Then the whole uv tree lands on the target in one internally-consistent transfer — venv and its interpreter always agree, because they came from the same source in the same pass. No Phase-7-builds-it / Phase-9-deletes-it race, because there's no Phase 7. The current bug is literally an artifact of `install_on_target` fighting the `--delete` home mirror.

Bonus: a **fresh** target gets a working pc-switcher purely from folder_sync — no uv bootstrap over SSH, no `install.sh` on the target, no GitHub token / network-to-github during sync. Those dependencies just disappear.

## What you'd actually change

1. Remove `.local/share/uv/tools/pcswitcher` and `.local/bin/pc-switcher` from `_RUNTIME_EXCLUDE_RELPATHS`.
2. Delete the `InstallOnTargetJob`, its orchestrator Phase 7 wiring, and its integration tests.
3. Keep `.local/share/pc-switcher` excluded — lock/history/logs are the genuinely machine-local state.
4. `install.sh` stays, but only as the manual bootstrap for **machine #1** (the first one you ever set up). Any machine that has *received* a sync already has pc-switcher and can sync *from* itself later.

## Caveats worth a conscious decision (not blockers)

- **Homogeneous-fleet assumption.** uv venvs use absolute-path shebangs + `pyvenv.cfg`. Relocatable only because both machines are `/home/janfr` + x86_64 (verified true now). `install_on_target` was path/arch-agnostic (it rebuilt locally); dropping it trades that robustness for simplicity. Fine for your fleet — but it becomes a documented invariant (ADR-worthy, and it touches ADR-016's "runtime excludes" rationale and whatever ADR justified install-on-target).
- **You lose the version-safety gate.** `validate()` currently aborts if the target's pc-switcher is *newer* than the source (protects against an old checkout misreading a newer target's `sync-history.json`). With a pure mirror, source always wins and versions converge over time. If you want, keep a *decoupled* compat check (the abort, without the install) — but it's an edge case (only bites mid-development from an un-updated machine).

Net: I'd do it. It removes an entire over-SSH install path plus its GitHub/token/network failure modes, and it's what makes the `.local/share/uv/` mirror coherent instead of self-sabotaging. The main thing to commit to explicitly is the same-home + same-arch invariant.

Want me to write it up as an issue (with the ADR implications), or draft the change?

message.content:
I like the simplification.
However, I see two complications:
- There is issue 126 that would need pc-switcher (or separate cli) to be on target DURING pc-switcher run. We didn't evaluate if that issue is really a good way to go. (Ask a subagent if we need to know now.) Simplifying now to bring it back later is not good.
- If I would have a machine with other architecture (mac?), we are screwed. That is totally not on the planning now. So best consider YAGNI for this one?

content.text: Both are fair. Let me get facts on #126 before opining on it — spawning a subagent to read the issue and assess the specific thing that matters: *timing*.

input.description: Assess issue 126 target-side needs
input.prompt:
You are investigating GitHub issue #126 in the repo `flaksit/pc-switcher` (use `gh issue view 126 --repo flaksit/pc-switcher --comments`, and `gh issue view 126 --json title,body,labels,comments,state`). Also look at any linked issues/PRs/ADRs it references.

Context for why I'm asking: pc-switcher is a source-orchestrated sync tool. Today it has an `InstallOnTargetJob` (src/pcswitcher/jobs/install_on_target.py) that runs `uv tool install` over SSH to install pc-switcher on the target machine early in the sync (orchestrator Phase 7), BEFORE the main folder transfer (Phase 9, folder_sync). We are considering DELETING that install job and instead letting folder_sync simply carry pc-switcher's uv venv to the target as ordinary files (it lives under /home/janfr which is already mirrored). Currently nothing executes `pc-switcher` on the target during a sync except the install job's own version check — all other target-side commands are primitives (rsync, btrfs, df, etc.).

The one thing that could break this plan: if some planned feature needs pc-switcher (or a separate CLI) to actually EXECUTE ON THE TARGET during a sync run. Issue #126 is suspected to be such a feature.

Your job — answer these precisely:
1. What does #126 actually propose? One-paragraph summary.
2. Does it require pc-switcher (or any project CLI/agent/daemon) to RUN on the target machine during a sync? Quote the relevant text.
3. TIMING is the crux: if yes, would that target-side component need to already EXIST on the target BEFORE the main folder transfer happens — i.e. could it NOT simply ride along in the folder sync? Explain your reasoning. (E.g. a target-side agent that coordinates the transfer itself must pre-exist; a post-transfer verification step could arrive with the sync.)
4. Has #126 been evaluated/decided, or is it still an open idea? Note labels (status:*, type:*), state, and any comments indicating a decision or a rejected/preferred approach.
5. Bottom line: if we remove InstallOnTargetJob now, would #126 as currently written force us to bring target-side provisioning back? Yes / No / Depends-on-unmade-decision, with a one-line why.

Do NOT change any files. This is read-only research. Report concisely — findings, not process. Your final message is consumed by me (the orchestrating assistant), not shown to the user, so give me the raw assessment.

aiTitle: Fix pc-switcher sync command not found error

content.text:
I have enough to answer precisely.

## Findings on issue #126 (`flaksit/pc-switcher`)

**Title:** "Unify source/target execution: use internal CLI instead of duplicate bash commands"

### 1. What #126 proposes
The project keeps duplicate implementations of the same operation: a Python code path for source/local execution and hand-written bash-command strings for target/remote execution over SSH. #126 proposes eliminating the duplication by building an **internal CLI** (`pc-switcher internal <command>`) exposing low-level sync primitives, and invoking that CLI on the target over SSH instead of raw bash. Motivation: single implementation per op, no behavioral drift, easier testing, better error handling, type safety, and escape from bash quoting/escaping pain. The canonical example is `_update_sync_history()` in `orchestrator.py`, which locally calls `record_role()` but remotely runs a bash string — proposed replacement: `pc-switcher internal record-role target --source=hostname --timestamp=...`.

### 2. Does it require pc-switcher to RUN on the target during a sync?
**Yes, explicitly.** The whole mechanism is executing the project's CLI on the target via SSH. Quoted: "Create an internal CLI with low-level sync commands executable over SSH, replacing raw bash with consistent Python code paths. Example transformation: … execute `pc-switcher internal record-role target --source=hostname --timestamp=...`." The body also states its premise: "Since pc-switcher installs on the target machine during sync initialization, leveraging it for unified execution could address this." So #126 is *built on the assumption that the install-on-target step exists* — it is essentially the feature the orchestrating question worries about.

### 3. Timing — must the target CLI pre-exist before the folder transfer?
**Depends on which operations get converted, and that scope is explicitly undecided in the issue.**

- The one *named* example, `_update_sync_history()` / `record-role`, runs at the very END of the orchestrator (`orchestrator.py:387`, after Phase 9 folder transfer and Phase 10 post-snapshots). That one alone could ride along with the folder sync — pc-switcher would be present by the time it runs.
- **But** other dual-implementation target-side ops run BEFORE the folder transfer (Phase 9). Verified in `orchestrator.py`:
  - Topology/history read: `cat {HISTORY_PATH}` over SSH at line 689 (preflight, pre-transfer).
  - Disk-space preflight: `check_disk_space(self._remote_executor, "/")` at line 876 (Phase 5, pre-transfer).
  - Config sync (Phase 8, line 495) also touches the target before Phase 9.

  If #126 converts any of those pre-transfer bash calls into `pc-switcher internal …`, the target CLI **must already exist on the target before the folder transfer** — it cannot ride along. The issue's own task list ("Audit codebase for divergence", "Evaluate applicability across cases", "Which operations genuinely require bash") shows the set of converted operations is an open design question, not settled.

### 4. Has it been evaluated/decided?
**Still an open idea — not decided.** State: **open**. Labels: `ai:needs-speckit` (tagged "Clear but too large — needs speckit workflow first"), `comp:core`, `type:refactor`. **No `status:*` label. No comments. No assignee. No linked ADR/PR** (the issue itself lists "Create an architectural decision record" as a *future* task). So there is no chosen or rejected approach on record — it's a raw proposal awaiting a speckit/ADR process.

### 5. Bottom line
**Depends-on-unmade-decision.** #126 as written assumes pc-switcher runs on the target and would need it present before Phase 9 *if* pre-transfer bash ops (history read, disk check, config sync) are among those converted — but exactly which ops get converted is an explicit open question in the issue, and the only concrete example (record-role) runs post-transfer and could ride along. Removing `InstallOnTargetJob` now does not contradict any *decided* requirement; it would only conflict with #126 if a future design chooses to route pre-transfer target operations through the internal CLI. Note that #126's stated premise leans on the install step existing, so deleting it should be recorded as a deliberate constraint that #126's eventual design must account for (e.g., converted commands limited to post-transfer, or a lightweight bootstrap).

attachment.stderr: Failed with non-blocking status code: /bin/sh: 1: /usr/bin/node: not found

content.text:
Subagent's read on #126: it's real and it cuts against the simplification. Summary of what matters:

- **#126 proposes exactly the thing you're worried about** — a `pc-switcher internal <cmd>` CLI invoked *on the target over SSH*, to kill the source-Python-vs-target-bash duplication. Its stated premise literally leans on "pc-switcher installs on the target during sync initialization."
- **Timing is the killer for some ops.** Its only concrete example (`record-role`) runs *post*-transfer and could ride along with folder_sync. But other dual-impl target ops run *before* Phase 9 — history/topology read at preflight, disk check (Phase 5), config sync (Phase 8). If #126 converts any of those, the target CLI must pre-exist the folder transfer → a venv delivered in Phase 9 is too late.
- **It's undecided.** Open, `ai:needs-speckit`, `type:refactor`, no ADR, no `status:`, scope of "which ops get converted" explicitly listed as an open question.

So this is precisely the "simplify now, rebuild later" churn you flagged. My verdict: **don't remove `install_on_target` now.** Not because #126 is committed — it isn't — but because removing the install step would silently constrain #126's design space (force every converted op to be post-transfer) before anyone's decided that's acceptable.

## The reframe that unblocks you today

The bug and the simplification are *separable* — we were conflating them. The bug isn't "install_on_target exists." The bug is that **uv's managed area is mirrored incoherently**: `.local/share/uv/python/` (interpreters) is `--delete`-mirrored, but the pcswitcher venv that depends on a specific interpreter is *excluded/frozen*. The mirror deletes the interpreter out from under the frozen venv.

Fix it the decision-neutral way: treat **all of `.local/share/uv/` as machine-local** — exclude it from the mirror, keep `install_on_target`. Rationale that stands on its own: uv interpreters are per-machine, patch-versioned, and re-downloaded on demand; `--delete`-mirroring them across machines is just wrong. This:

- fixes the reported breakage,
- is neutral to #126 (install step stays),
- and is *more* arch-robust, not less — `install_on_target` rebuilds the venv locally, so it never cares about the source's arch.

Tradeoff to name explicitly: your *other* uv tools (awscli, basedpyright, …) currently propagate across machines by accident via the mirror. Excluding `.local/share/uv/` stops that. But that's the job of the planned **packages** sync feature (your config already has `sync_jobs: packages: true`) — declarative reinstall, not file-mirroring a per-arch tree. So this is aligned with the roadmap, and it removes a latent bomb: every one of those tools has the same interpreter-deletion failure waiting to happen.

## On Mac / other arch

Agree — pure YAGNI, don't build for it. Note the payoff: the conservative fix above (keep `install_on_target`, don't mirror uv) is exactly the arch-*robust* one. It's the *simplification* (mirror the venv) that would have hard-coupled you to same-arch. So your two concerns point the same direction.

Cheap insurance worth adding regardless: a preflight that compares source vs target `uname -m` and aborts with a clear message on mismatch. ~free, and it converts "someday a Mac produces a silently dangling symlink" (today's debugging session, again) into a loud, honest error. That's the YAGNI-compatible move — refuse the case you don't support instead of half-handling it.

Net recommendation: keep `install_on_target`; fix the bug by excluding `.local/share/uv/` from the folder mirror; add the arch-match preflight; park the "carry the venv, drop install_on_target" simplification as a note on #126 for whenever its design is actually decided.
